questions.text: Lymph nodes are not present:
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Lymph nodes are present in neck region, axilla, and groin of humans. Lymph node is a mass of connective tissue. Several afferent lymph vessels enter lymph node which is emptied into single efferent lymph vessel. Spleen is a lymphoid mass.

questions.text: Major coordinating centre for sensory and motor signaling is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Thalamus is a major coordinating centre for sensory and motor signalling. The thalamus acts as a sensory relay in visual, auditory, somatosensory, and gustatory systems,
Cerebrum functions in the analysis and interpretation of memory, reasoning, judgement, thoughts and dreams.
Hypothalamus maintains homeostasis and contains centres for regulating hunger, thirst, temperature, water balance etc.
Cerebellum controls equilibrium.

questions.explanation: Thalamus is mass of grey matter located at the base of cerebrum in the wall of third ventricle. It serves as relay station for sensory impulses as well as integration centre for recognition of pain, temperature and hard touch.

questions.text: The system involved in preparing our body in condition of stress and emergency:
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: The sympathetic nervous system is involved in preparing the body for stress-related activities; the parasympathetic nervous system is associated with returning the body to routine, day-to-day operations. These divisions of nervous systems when stimulated, activate the organs and glands in the endocrine system. These glands respond by pumping the hormone epinephrine (also known as adrenaline) into the bloodstream.

questions.text: What part of the brain integrates sensory information such as pain and temperature?
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: The forebrain contains the cerebral cortex and a number of other structures that lie beneath the cortex (called subcortical structures): thalamus, hypothalamus, pituitary gland, and the limbic system (collection of structures). The parietal lobe is behind the frontal lobe, separated by the central sulcus. Areas in the parietal lobe are responsible for integrating sensory information, including touch, temperature, pressure and pain.

questions.text: The autonomic nervous system controls all of the following activities except:
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Thought is a conscious action so it is not controlled by the autonomic nervous system rather by the somatic nervous system. The autonomic nervous system regulates certain body processes, such as blood pressure and the rate of breathing. This system works automatically (autonomously), without a person's conscious effort.

questions.text: The hormone which controls the uptake of Sodium ions in kidney and its maintenance in blood plasma is:
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Aldosterone is a steroid hormone. Its main role is to regulate salt and water in the body, thus having an effect on blood pressure. Growth hormone (GH), also called somatotropin or human growth hormone, is a peptide hormone secreted by the anterior lobe of the pituitary gland. It stimulates the growth of essentially all tissues of the body, including bone. Gonadotropins: Luteinizing and Follicle Stimulating Hormones. Luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) are called gonadotropins because they stimulate the gonads - in males, the testes, and in females, the ovaries. Thyroxine is the main hormone secreted into the bloodstream by the thyroid gland. It plays vital roles in digestion, heart and muscle function, brain development and maintenance of bones.

questions.text: Four events in the transmission of nerve impulses across synapses are: I. depolarisation of the presynaptic membrane II. propagation of postsynaptic action potential III. reabsorption of the transmitter substance IV. release of transmitter substance into the synaptic cleft In which sequence do these events occur? FIRST -> LAST
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: The sequence of transmission of nerve impulse is as follows:1.Depolarisation occurs at the presynaptic terminal.2.This leads to the release of the transmitter substance to the cleft.3.This leads to the depolarization at the postsynaptic membrane.4.Then the transmitter substance is reabsorbed back to the presynaptic membrane.

questions.text: Which of these is not a reflex action?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Secretion of sweat is not a reflex action. It is controlled by the autonomic nervous system. Hence, this option is the correct option.
Salivation is the secretion of saliva in the mouth when it comes in contact with food or smells food. It is a reflex action. Hence, the option is incorrect.
Flexion is the act of withdrawing the stimulated part of the body when the needle pricks and stimulates pain receptors. It is a reflex action. Hence, the option is incorrect.

options.text: Blinking of eyes due to strong light
options.explanation: In strong light, we blink our eyes due to the stimulation of light-sensitive cells. It is a reflex action. Hence, the option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Reflex actions are quick, automatic responses to some stimuli that are not generated by the brain but by the spinal cord.

questions.text: As an essential element in living organisms, _ ion is playing an important role in insulin secretion, the release of neurotransmitters, muscle contraction, and heartbeat regulation.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sodium is an important ion in the body, but it is not involved in insulin secretion or the release of neurotransmitters.
This option is also incorrect. Potassium ions are an essential element in living organisms, but it is not involved in insulin secretion or the release of neurotransmitters.

Calcium ion plays an important role in the secretion of neurotransmitters and insulin, as when calcium ion moves against the concentration gradient into the cells they stimulate Microtubule elongation making the Vesicles move toward the membrane and eventually expel exocytose out. Some ions bind to Troponin molecules making them displace hydrophobic proteins such as Troponin and Tropomyosin to displace away.
Chloride ions are important for maintaining fluid balance and pH in the body, but they are not directly involved in insulin secretion or the release of neurotransmitters.

questions.explanation: Calcium ion plays an important role in the secretion of neurotransmitters and insulin, as when calcium ion moves against the concentration gradient into the cells they stimulate microtubule elongation making the vesicles move towards the membrane and eventually expel exocytose out. Same ions can bind to Troponin molecules making them displace hydrophobic proteins such as troponin and tropomyosin to displace away.

questions.text: _ is sometimes given by injection as an emergency treatment in cardiac arrest:
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: When CPR does not generate coronary perfusion of more than 15-20 mmHg, return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) rarely or never occurs. After more than a few minutes of cardiac arrest, arterial tone collapses and epinephrine or another vasoconstrictor is essential for restoration of cardiac activity.

questions.text: Which one of the following is common to all neurons?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A cell body which contains a nucleus
options.explanation: The cell body or soma is the main nutritional part of the cell and is concerned with the biosynthesis of materials necessary for the growth and maintenance of the neuron. Hence is present in all neurons.

The cell body or soma is the main nutritional part of the cell and is concerned with the biosynthesis of materials necessary for the growth and maintenance of the neuron. Hence is present in all neurons. Some neurons are non-myelinated, meaning they will lack Nodes of Ranvier.

The cell body or soma is the main nutritional part of the cell and is concerned with the biosynthesis of materials necessary for the growth and maintenance of the neuron, hence is present in all neurons. Not all neurons have a thick myelin sheath. Neurons have a range of thickness in myelin sheaths, defined based on how fast must a particular neuron conduct an impulse.

The cell body or soma is the main nutritional part of the cell and is concerned with the biosynthesis of materials necessary for the growth and maintenance of the neuron hence is present in all neurons. Some neurons are non-myelinated, meaning they will lack Schwann cells.

questions.explanation: The cell body or soma is the main nutritional part of the cell and is concerned with the biosynthesis of materials necessary for the growth and maintenance of the neuron hence is present in all neurons.

questions.text: The sympathetic nervous system causes which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is a relaxed state and a function of the parasympathetic nervous system.
This is also a function of the parasympathetic system.
This is also a function of the parasympathetic nervous system.
Adrenaline secretion is decreased during relaxed conditions i.e. parasympathetic nervous system.
Respiration is increased during some emergency conditions and this is handled by the sympathetic nervous system.

questions.explanation: The sympathetic nervous system mostly comes into play during times when the body feels it needs to respond to threatening stimuli. This response is called the fight-or-flight response. During a threatening situation, this system will respond by increasing heart rate,increasing respiration, activate sweat glands, increase blood flow, and dilate the pupils. It slows bodily processes that are less important in emergencies such as digestion.

questions.text: _ hormone is released from the posterior lobe of the pituitary gland.
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: The posterior lobe of the pituitary gland produces two hormones, vasopressin (Antidiuretic hormone) and oxytocin. Adrenaline is released from the adrenal glands and its major action, together with noradrenaline, is to prepare the body for 'fight or flight'. Thyroid-stimulating hormone, also known as TSH, is a glycoprotein hormone produced by the anterior pituitary. Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) is released by the anterior lobe of pituitary gland.

questions.text: Myelin sheath of neuron is composed of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Myelin sheath is made of sphingolipids.
Myelin sheath is not made of choline.
Myelin sheath is not made of ethanolamine.
Myelin sheath is not made of waxes.

questions.explanation: The myelin sheath of neurons is composed of sphingolipids. Ethanolamine and choline are nitrogenous bases found in phospholipids, Waxes are abundantly found in protective covering of fruits and leaves.

questions.text: The plant hormone responsible for fruit ripening is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Abscisic acid (ABA) is involved in various plant responses, including seed dormancy, stomatal closure, and stress responses, but it is not primarily responsible for fruit ripening.
Auxins are involved in plant growth and development, including cell elongation, tropisms, and apical dominance. However, they are not the primary hormone responsible for fruit ripening.

Cytokinins are involved in cell division and growth, bud activation, and delay of senescence. While they can have some influence on fruit development, they are not the main hormone responsible for fruit ripening.

The plant hormone responsible for fruit ripening is ethylene. Ethylene is a gaseous plant hormone that plays a crucial role in the regulation of various physiological processes, including the ripening of fruits. It acts as a signaling molecule, triggering and coordinating the processes involved in fruit ripening.
Gibberellins are involved in stem elongation, seed germination, and flowering. They can affect fruit development but are not primarily responsible for fruit ripening.

questions.explanation: The plant hormone responsible for fruit ripening is ethylene. Ethylene is a gaseous plant hormone that plays a crucial role in the regulation of various physiological processes, including the ripening of fruits. It acts as a signaling molecule, triggering and coordinating the processes involved in fruit ripening.

questions.text: The prolactin hormone, responsible for the activation of mammary glands to start producing milk, is a hormone of the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Prolactin is a peptide hormone secreted by the lactotroph cells in the anterior lobe of the pituitary gland. Therefore, option A is the correct answer.
Option B is incorrect because the pancreas produces insulin and Glucagon, but not prolactin.
Option C is incorrect because the thyroid gland is responsible for producing T3 and T4.
Option D is incorrect because the thymus gland releases several hormones, including thymosin.
Option E is incorrect because the adrenal gland produces a wide variety of hormones such as cortisol, aldosterone androgens, adrenaline, epinephrine, and norepinephrine.

questions.explanation: In humans, prolactin is produced both in the front portion of the pituitary gland (anterior pituitary gland) and in a range of sites elsewhere in the body. Lactotroph cells in the pituitary gland produce prolactin, where it is stored and then released into the bloodstream.

questions.text: Fundography is relevant to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Terminologies related to heart use the words cardiac or coronary.
Terminologies related to liver use the word hepatic.
Terminologies related to stomach use the word gastric.

Terminologies related to eyes use the word optics but fundus is also a part of the eye and words including 'fundus' are related to eyes. Similarly fundography involves taking a photograph of the fundus of the eye.

questions.explanation: Fundography uses a photograph of the inside surface of your eye – including the retina, optic disc, macula and posterior pole (fundus) – to diagnose or monitor the progression of eye disease.

questions.text: Thyroid gland requires high amount of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phosphate is involved in bone and teeth formation.
Calcium is involved in bone formation and muscle contraction.
Iodine is a component of thyroid hormones Triiodothyronine and Tetraiodothyronine.
Sodium is involved in nerve impulse conduction.

questions.explanation: Iodine is essential to maintain a healthy thyroid. Iodine is the critical 'ingredient' for the production of thyroid hormones.

questions.text: Which of the following is not the function of the cerebrum?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A is incorrect as the functions of the cerebrum include interpreting memory, reasoning, judgment, and thoughts as well as coordinating movement. It is not involved in digestion. Additionally, digestion is not a voluntary process.
The cerebrum is heavily involved in thinking processes. It is responsible for complex cognitive functions, including reasoning, problem-solving, decision-making, and creativity.
Intelligence is closely related to the functions of the cerebrum. The cerebrum, particularly the cerebral cortex, is responsible for higher-order thinking and intellectual abilities.
The cerebrum is involved in the voluntary control of skeletal muscles. It sends signals through the motor cortex to initiate and coordinate voluntary movements.

questions.explanation: Functions of the cerebrum include initiation of movement, coordination of movement, temperature, touch, vision, hearing, judgment, reasoning, problem-solving, emotions, and learning, which do not involve digestion. Digestion is controlled by the autonomic nervous system- it is not a voluntary function!

questions.text: Which of the following is the function of adrenaline?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: To increase the rate of breathing
options.explanation: Adrenaline causes bronchial dilation which results in faster breathing.
Adrenaline causes vasoconstriction which leads to an increase in heart rate.

options.text: To increase the level of calcium in blood
options.explanation: The level of calcium in the blood is increased by the action of parathormone.
Both option A and B are correct.

questions.explanation: Adrenaline is released in a fight or flight situation. In flight or fight conditions, both the breathing rate and the heart rate increase in order to increase alertness.

questions.text: Hepatic and Pancreatic secretions are also stimulated by a hormone called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gastrin controls stomach secretions.
Secretin controls liver and pancreas secretions.
Insulin decreases blood glucose.
Glucagon increases blood glucose.

questions.explanation: Secretin is a hormone that regulates water homeostasis throughout the body and influences the environment of the duodenum by regulating secretions in the stomach, pancreas, and liver.

questions.text: The spinal nerves are functionally:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A is incorrect as sensory nerves are not mixed nerves.
Option B is incorrect as motor nerves are not mixed nerves.
Option C is correct as spinal nerves contain both motor neurons and sensory neurons so functionally they are called mixed nerve.
Option D is incorrect as unknown is not the answer.

questions.explanation: The cell bodies of the sensory neurons are in the dorsal root ganglion, but the motor neuron cell bodies are in the grey matter. The two roots join to form the spinal nerve just before the nerve leaves the vertebral column. Because all spinal nerves have both sensory and motor components, they are mixed nerves.

questions.text: Deficient production of hormones by adrenal glands results in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cushing's syndrome is a disorder that occurs when your body makes too much of the hormone cortisol over a long period of time.
Option B is the correct answer. Addison's disease, also called adrenal insufficiency, is an uncommon illness that occurs when the body doesn't make enough of certain hormones.

Diabetes mellitus refers to a group of diseases that affect how the body uses blood sugar (glucose). Diabetes can cause heart attack, heart failure, stroke, kidney failure and coma. These complications can lead to your death.
A goiter is the irregular growth of the thyroid gland. The thyroid is a butterfly-shaped gland located at the base of the neck just below the Adam's apple.
Epilepsy is a central nervous system (neurological) disorder in which brain activity becomes abnormal, causing seizures or periods of unusual behavior, sensations and sometimes loss of awareness.

questions.explanation: When adrenal glands don't produce enough hormones, this can lead to adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease).

questions.text: The region where the impulse moves from one neuron to another is called _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Axon is a long cytoplasmic process which usually arises opposite to dendrite. It is the conducting end of a neuron.
Dendrites receive stimuli and convey it to soma.
Synapse is the region where the impulse moves from one neuron to another. It consists of three compartments; a presynaptic membrane, a narrow space called synaptic cleft and a postsynaptic membrane.
Thalamus serves as the main relay station for the brain. Motor pathways, limbic pathways, and sensory pathways besides olfaction all pass through this central structure.
Cerebellum plays a role in motor movement regulation and balance control.

questions.explanation: Synapse, also called neuronal junction, the site of transmission of electric nerve impulses between two nerve cells (neurons) or between a neuron and a gland or muscle cell (effector).

questions.text: What are the functions of the inter, motor and sensory neurons in a reflex response?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Functions of sensory neurons Sensory neurons are the nerve cells that are activated by sensory input from the environment - for example, when you touch a hot surface with your fingertips, the sensory neurons will be the ones firing and sending off signals to the rest of the nervous system about the information they have received. Functions of Motor neurons Motor neurons of the spinal cord are part of the central nervous system (CNS) and connect to muscles, glands, and organs throughout the body. These neurons transmit impulses from the spinal cord to skeletal and smooth muscles (such as those in your stomach), and so directly control all of our muscle movements. Functions of interneurons As the name suggests, interneurons are the ones in between - they connect the spinal motor and sensory neurons. As well as transferring signals between sensory and motor neurons, interneurons can also communicate with each other, forming circuits of various complexity.
This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: To relay nerve impulses from within the CNS, interneurons are used. To conduct the nerve impulses from the CNS to the effector, motor neurons are used. To conduct the nerve impulses from the receptors to the CNS, sensory neurons are used.

questions.text: Four events in the transmission of nerve impulses across synapses are:I. depolarisation of the presynaptic membraneII. propagation of postsynaptic action potentialIII. reabsorption of the transmitter substanceIV. release of transmitter substance into the synaptic cleftIn which sequence do these events occur? FIRST -> LAST
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.

The sequence of transmission of nerve impulses is as follows; Depolarisation of the presynaptic membrane occurs. As a result of the depolarisation of the presynaptic membrane, the neurotransmitter is released into the synaptic cleft. The neurotransmitter binds to the receptors on the post-synaptic membrane, which generates action potential in the post-synaptic cell. The neurotransmitter is then reabsorbed by the pre-synaptic cells for reuse. So, from the above summary, it shows that the sequence in the transmission of nerve impulse across synapses is 1, 4, 2 and then 3.
As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.
As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.
As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The sequence of transmission of nerve impulses is as follows: 1. Depolarisation occurs at the presynaptic terminal. 2. This leads to the release of the transmitter substance to the cleft. 3. This leads to the depolarization at the postsynaptic membrane. 4. Then the transmitter substance is reabsorbed back to the presynaptic membrane. So, from the above summary, it shows that the sequence in the transmission of nerve impulses across synapses is 1, 4, 2, and then 3.

questions.text: How many meninges cover the human brain?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.

Both the brain and spinal cord are covered by three membranes, collectively known as Meninges, the outer hard ( next to the cranium ), the inner Pia matter ( next to the brain and spinal cord), and the middle arachnoid matter.
This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: There are three layers of meninges around the brain and spinal cord. The outer layer, the dura mater, is tough, white fibrous connective tissue. The middle layer of meninges is the arachnoid, a thin layer resembling a cobweb with numerous threadlike strands attaching it to the innermost layer

questions.text: In an experiment, the production of hormone secretin was blocked. As a result, levels of all of the following enzymes were affected EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Trypsin is an enzyme produced by the pancreas. It is involved in the breakdown of proteins in the small intestine. Since secretin regulates the secretion of pancreatic enzymes, including trypsin, it is likely that the levels of trypsin would be affected when secretin production is blocked. Therefore, trypsin would be affected.

Pepsin is not directly regulated by secretin, so its levels would not be affected when secretin production is blocked. Pepsin is an enzyme that is produced in the stomach and helps in the digestion of proteins. Secretin does not directly regulate the production of pepsin. It primarily influences the secretion of pancreatic enzymes. Thus, the levels of pepsin would not be affected when secretin production is blocked. Therefore, pepsin would not be affected.

Chymotrypsin is another pancreatic enzyme involved in the digestion of proteins. As with trypsin, secretin regulates the secretion of chymotrypsin, so its production would likely be affected when secretin production is blocked. Therefore, chymotrypsin would be affected.

Amylase is an enzyme responsible for the breakdown of starches and carbohydrates. It is also produced by the pancreas and its secretion is influenced by secretin. Therefore, the levels of amylase would be affected when secretin production is blocked. Therefore, amylase would be affected.

Lipase is an enzyme that breaks down fats in the small intestine. Like trypsin and chymotrypsin, lipase is produced by the pancreas and its secretion is regulated by secretin. Therefore, the levels of lipase would be affected when secretin production is blocked. Therefore, lipase would be affected.

questions.explanation: Secretin is involved in triggering the release of all the enzymes listed above except pepsin. Pepsin is not directly regulated by secretin, so its levels would not be affected when secretin production is blocked.

questions.text: The gland known as the “gland of emergency” is the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The pituitary gland is called the Master gland because of the influence of its hormones on other endocrine glands.

Adrenal glands are termed glands of emergency. It secretes adrenaline (epinephrine) and nor-adrenaline ( nor-epinephrine) under conditions of stress to bring about a fight-or-flight response in emergency situations. That’s why it is called the “gland of emergency”.
It has no specific name.
It has no specific name.
Pancreas is called Islets of Langerhans.

questions.explanation: The adrenal gland located at the top of the kidneys is involved in the release of adrenaline which is an emergency in flight-or-fight conditions.

questions.text: The autonomic nervous system controls all of the following activities except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: he autonomic nervous system (ANS) plays a crucial role in the regulation of digestion. It controls the smooth muscle contractions in the digestive tract, secretion of digestive enzymes, and the release of hormones involved in digestion. Therefore, digestion of food is indeed controlled by the autonomic nervous system.

The autonomic nervous system is responsible for regulating the heartbeat. It controls the rate and force of the heart's contractions through a balance between the sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions of the ANS.

The autonomic nervous system controls the constriction and dilation of the pupil of the eye. The circular muscles of the iris, known as the pupillary sphincter, are innervated by the parasympathetic division of the ANS.

The autonomic nervous system does not directly control thought processes. Thought is primarily regulated by the central nervous system, which includes the brain and spinal cord. While the autonomic nervous system can be influenced by certain thought patterns, such as stress or relaxation, it does not directly control the generation or content of thoughts. Hence, option C is correct.

The autonomic nervous system controls the breathing rate by regulating the muscles involved in respiration. The respiratory centers in the brainstem receive inputs from various sensors, including chemoreceptors that detect the levels of oxygen, carbon dioxide, and pH in the blood.

questions.explanation: The autonomic nervous system does not directly control thought processes. Thought is primarily regulated by the central nervous system, which includes the brain and spinal cord. While the autonomic nervous system can be influenced by certain thought patterns, such as stress or relaxation, it does not directly control the generation or content of thoughts.

questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In the resting membrane, the outside is positively charged because of the high concentration of Na+ ions, and the inside is negatively charged because of the high concentration of inorganic ions than the concentration of K+ ions. But the concentration of K+ ions is still high inside the membrane as compared to Na+ ions, so it is correct.

But here concentration if K+ ions is still high inside the memberane as compared to Na+ ions. In given figure concentration of K+ ions is low. So, it is incorrect.

Moreover, the concentration of Na+ ions is less outside. So, it is incorrect.

questions.text: Match the parts of the human brain listed under Column I with the functions given under Column II. Choose the answer which gives the correct combination of alphabets of the two columns.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Your cerebral hemisphere plays a key role in memory, intelligence, thinking, learning, reasoning, problem-solving, emotions, consciousness, and functions related to your senses so A=T. The thalamus is your body's information relay station. All information from your body's senses (except smell) must be processed through your thalamus before being sent to your brain's cerebral cortex for interpretation, so B=P. This makes option A incorrect.

Your cerebral hemisphere plays a key role in memory, intelligence, thinking, learning, reasoning, problem-solving, emotions, consciousness, and functions related to your senses so A=T. The thalamus is your body's information relay station. All information from your body's senses (except smell) must be processed through your thalamus before being sent to your brain's cerebral cortex for interpretation, so B=P. This makes option B incorrect.

Your cerebral hemisphere plays a key role in memory, intelligence, thinking, learning, reasoning, problem-solving, emotions, consciousness, and functions related to your senses so A=T. The thalamus is your body's information relay station. All information from your body's senses (except smell) must be processed through your thalamus before being sent to your brain's cerebral cortex for interpretation, so B=P. The cerebellum controls voluntary movements such as walking, posture, balance, coordination, eye movements, and speech (C=Q). Neurons in the medulla also control arousal and sleep (D=R).

All information from your body's senses (except smell) must be processed through your thalamus before being sent to your brain's cerebral cortex for interpretation, so B=P. This makes option D incorrect.

questions.explanation: Your cerebral hemisphere plays a key role in memory, intelligence, thinking, learning, reasoning, problem-solving, emotions, consciousness, and functions related to your senses. (so A=T, hence options A and B are incorrect). The thalamus is your body's information relay station. All information from your body's senses (except smell) must be processed through your thalamus before being sent to your brain's cerebral cortex for interpretation (so B=P, hence options A, B, and D are incorrect). The cerebellum controls voluntary movements such as walking, posture, balance, coordination, eye movements, and speech (C=Q). Neurons in the medulla also control arousal and sleep (D=R).

questions.text: Which of the following neurotransmitters function, both as neurotransmitter and hormones, decreasing our perception of pain?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Epinephrine, commonly referred to as Adrenaline, relaxes the airway muscles, making it easier for the person to breathe. At the same time, it constricts blood vessels, helping to improve blood pressure. It also increases the heart rate. However, it does not decrease the response to pain. Hence Option A is rejected.

Serotonin, on the other hand, is used by the body to send messages between nerve cells. It appears to play a role in mood, emotions, appetite, and digestion. As the precursor for melatonin, it helps regulate sleep-wake cycles and the body clock. Since it is not used to decrease pain response, Option B is rejected.

Dopamine is a neurotransmitter that plays a role in how we feel pleasure. It's a big part of our unique human ability to think and plan. However, it plays no role in the pain response. Hence Option C is rejected.

Endorphins are substances produced by the nervous system to cope with pain or stress. They are often called “feel-good” chemicals because they can act as pain relievers and happiness boosters. Hence, Option D is correct.

questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters are chemical messengers that transmit a message from a nerve cell across the synapse to a target cell. Hormones are chemical messengers that are secreted directly into the blood, which carries them to organs and tissues of the body to exert their functions. All the options are both, a hormone as well neurotransmitters. Epinephrine, commonly referred to as Adrenaline, relaxes the airway muscles, making it easier for the person to breathe. At the same time, it constricts blood vessels, helping to improve blood pressure. It also increases the heart rate. However, it does not decrease the response to pain. Hence Option A is rejected. Serotonin, on the other hand, is used by the body to send messages between nerve cells. It appears to play a role in mood, emotions, appetite, and digestion. As the precursor for melatonin, it helps regulate sleep-wake cycles and the body clock. Since it is not used to decrease pain response, Option B is rejected. Dopamine is a neurotransmitter that plays a role in how we feel pleasure. It's a big part of our unique human ability to think and plan. However, it plays no role in the pain response. Hence Option C is rejected. Endorphins are substances produced by the nervous system to cope with pain or stress. They are often called “feel-good” chemicals because they can act as pain relievers and happiness boosters. Hence, Option D is correct.

questions.text: Which body function is controlled through a positive feedback mechanism?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: An example of positive feedback is uterine contractions during childbirth. The hormone oxytocin, made by the endocrine system, stimulates the contraction of the uterus. This produces the pain sensed by the nervous system. In response, more oxytocin is released which in turn stimulates the contractions even more. This loop of the 'pain induced from contractions stimulating the release of oxytocin, and oxytocin stimulating more contraction continues with increasing intensity' still the fetus is birthed. This is a positive feedback loop hence, Option A is correct.
Thermoregulation (if body temperature changes, mechanisms are induced to restore normal levels). It is a negative feedback mechanism.
Blood sugar regulation (insulin lowers blood glucose when levels are high; glucagon raises blood glucose when levels are low). It is a negative feedback mechanism.

Thyroxine Regulation. The two scenarios involved with thyroxine regulation are stated below:1) Low thyroxine levels in the bloodstream stimulate the hypothalamus to release TRH (Thyrotropin-releasing hormone) and this causes the pituitary to release TSH (Thyroid-stimulating hormone) so, the thyroid releases more thyroxine and the blood levels return to normal. 2) Normal thyroxine levels in the bloodstream inhibit TRH release from the hypothalamus and this inhibits the release of TSH from the pituitary, so less thyroxine is released from the thyroid gland and normal blood levels are maintained. It is a negative feedback mechanism.

questions.explanation: A feedback mechanism is a loop system wherein the system responds to a perturbation. The response may be in the same direction (as in positive feedback) or in the opposite direction (as in negative feedback). An example of positive feedback is uterine contractions during childbirth. The hormone oxytocin, made by the endocrine system, stimulates the contraction of the uterus. This produces the pain sensed by the nervous system. In response, more oxytocin is released which in turn stimulates the contractions even more. This loop of the 'pain induced from contractions stimulating the release of oxytocin, and oxytocin stimulating more contraction continues with increasing intensity' still the fetus is birthed. This is a positive feedback loop hence, Option A is correct.

questions.text: Which of the following is not secreted by thyroid gland?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Thyroxin: Thyroxin, also known as T4, is a hormone secreted by the thyroid gland. Its principal function is to stimulate the consumption of oxygen and thus the metabolism of all cells and tissues in the body.

Triiodothyronine: Triiodothyronine, or T3, is a thyroid hormone that plays vital roles in the body’s metabolic rate, heart and digestive functions, muscle control, brain development and function, and the maintenance of bones. It is released by the thyroid gland along with thyroxin (T4).
Calcitonin: Calcitonin is a hormone that your thyroid gland makes to help regulate calcium levels in blood by decreasing it. The C-cells in thyroid gland produce calcitonin.

Glucagon: This statement is correct. Glucagon is not secreted by the thyroid gland. It is a hormone that the body naturally produces, specifically in an organ called the pancreas. Its main function is to increase blood glucose levels.

questions.explanation: Glucagon: Glucagon is not secreted by the thyroid gland. It is a hormone that the body naturally produces, specifically in an organ called the pancreas. Its main function is to increase blood glucose levels. It is secreted by alpha cells.

questions.text: The phytohormones controlling the plant movements are?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Auxins are a group of plant hormones that play a significant role in controlling various plant growth and developmental processes, including phototropism (bending of plant towards light), gravitropism (response to gravity), and apical dominance (control of shoot growth). They influence the elongation of cells, which is crucial for plant movements.

Gibberellins are another group of plant hormones that are involved in regulating plant growth and development. They influence stem elongation, seed germination, and flowering. In some cases, they can also affect tropic responses, such as gravitropism and phototropism.

Abscisic acid (ABA) is a plant hormone that plays a vital role in various stress responses and in regulating the closure of stomata. Stomatal closure is a movement that helps in preventing water loss during periods of water stress or drought conditions.

Cytokinins are plant hormones that are responsible for promoting cell division and growth. While they are not directly involved in movements like tropisms, their role in cell division influences overall plant growth, which, in turn, can affect the direction and extent of plant movements.

All of these phytohormones (auxins, gibberellins, abscisic acid, and cytokinins) play significant roles in controlling different aspects of plant growth, development, and responses to environmental stimuli. Together, they contribute to various plant movements and their overall growth and adaptation to their surroundings.

questions.explanation: The five major phytohormones are – auxins, gibberellins, cytokinins, ethylene and abscisic acid Auxin is an essential molecule that controls almost every aspect of plant development. It is also concerned with apical dominance. Gibberellins are plant growth regulators that facilitate cell elongation, help the plants to grow taller. Abscisic acid (ABA) is a plant hormone that regulates numerous aspects of plant growth, development, and stress responses. Cytokinins are a class of plant hormones that promote cell division, or cytokinesis, in plant roots and shoots. They are involved primarily in cell growth and differentiation, but also affect apical dominance, axillary bud growth, and leaf senescence.

questions.text: Human cells maintain concentration gradients across their plasma membranes, such that there is a high sodium concentration outside the cell and a high potassium concentration inside the cell. Suppose that within the cell membrane are sodium leak channels or carrier proteins. These channels would allow sodium to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Move out of the cell by simple diffusion
options.explanation: Option A is wrong because neither sodium moves out of the cell nor it transports via simple diffusion.

options.text: Move into the cell by simple diffusion
options.explanation: Option B is wrong because sodium does not transport via simple diffusion.

options.text: Move out of the cell by facilitated diffusion
options.explanation: Option C is wrong because sodium moves inside the cell, not out of the cell.

options.text: Move into the cell by facilitated diffusion
options.explanation: As the question mentions there is a high concentration of sodium ions outside the cell, that means through sodium leaky channels the sodium ions should move inside the cell not outside. The type of movement that occurs through channels or carrier proteins down the concentration gradient is called facilitated diffusion. That is why, the answer is D.

questions.explanation: As the question mentions there is a high concentration of sodium ions outside the cell, that means through sodium leaky channels the sodium ions should move inside the cell not outside. The type of movement that occurs through channels or carrier proteins down the concentration gradient is called facilitated diffusion. That is why, the answer is D.

questions.text: Which hormone prepares the body for situations of stress and emergency?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Adrenaline triggers the body's fight-or-flight response. This reaction causes air passages to dilate to provide the muscles with the oxygen they need to either fight danger or flee. Adrenaline also triggers the blood vessels to contract to re-direct blood toward major muscle groups, including the heart and lungs.

Together with adrenaline, noradrenaline increases heart rate and blood pumping from the heart. It also increases blood pressure and helps break down fat and increase blood sugar levels to provide more energy to the body.

Thyroxine controls how much energy your body uses (the metabolic rate). It's also involved in digestion, how your heart and muscles work, brain development and bone health. When the thyroid gland does not make enough thyroxine (called hypothyroidism), many of the body's functions slow down.

Insulin is a peptide hormone secreted by the β cells of the pancreatic islets of Langerhans and maintains normal blood glucose levels by facilitating cellular glucose uptake, regulating carbohydrate, lipid and protein metabolism and promoting cell division and growth through its mitogenic effects.

questions.explanation: Adrenaline triggers the body's fight-or-flight response. This reaction causes air passages to dilate to provide the muscles with the oxygen they need to either fight danger or flee. Adrenaline also triggers the blood vessels to contract to re-direct blood toward major muscle groups, including the heart and lungs.

questions.text: Least distance vision for a person with hypermetropia is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option suggests that the least distance vision for a person with hypermetropia is 25 cm. However, this is incorrect. Hypermetropic individuals typically have difficulty focusing on objects closer than this distance due to the refractive error. Their near point is further away.

This option suggests that the least distance vision for a person with hypermetropia is less than 25 cm. This is also incorrect. Hypermetropia causes difficulty in focusing on close objects, so the near point is beyond 25 cm.

C is the correct option because the least distance of distinct vision is 35cm. This is more than the least distance of distinct vision of an ordinary person. Hence the persom is suffering from Hypermetropia.

This option suggests that the least distance vision for a person with hypermetropia is infinity. While it is true that hypermetropes have better distance vision compared to near vision, it is not accurate to say that their least distance vision is infinite. The near point is a finite distance, just farther away than for individuals with normal vision.

questions.explanation: The closest distance we can see with our eye is 25 cm. This is called the least distance of distinct vision which is the closest distance the lens of our eye can focus light on the retina. Now, if the closest distance a person can see is 35 cm then the least distance of distinct vision of the person has increased. This is called hypermetropia or farsightedness where the person's least distance for distinct vision is increased and he will have difficulty seeing things in the near but he will not have any problem to see the things in the far side. This condition happens because the image is formed behind the retina, not on the retina. Here, the eye lens focuses the incoming light from an object at a point behind the retina. A converging lens is useful to see his surroundings better. If we place a converging lens or convex lens between the eye and the object with the required converging power then the light rays from the object will focus at the retina. That’s why a converging lens is used to see properly if someone has hypermetropia.

questions.text: The nerve center for sight is located in the:

options.explanation: A is correct option because thalamus receives sensory information (i.e. electrical impulses) from the retina through the optic nerve. The retina is located at the back of eyeball, and it contains nerve cells. When light strikes these nerve cells, the nerve cells will send electrical impulses through the optic nerve to the thalamus. Once the thalamus receives these electrical impulses, the thalamus will send these impulses to the cortex
Option B is wrong because it is simply short sighted.
Option C is wrong because B is wrong.
Option D is wrong because option A is correct.

questions.explanation: In the visual system, the thalamus receives input from the retina, which is relayed to the brain via the optic nerve. B simply means short sighted hence incorrect.

questions.text: The sense organs of taste in tongue are known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A is wrong because Olfactory receptors (ORs), also known as odorant receptors, are chemoreceptors expressed in the cell membranes of olfactory receptor neurons and are responsible for the detection of odorants (for example, compounds that have an odor) which give rise to the sense of smell.

B is correct option because Gustatory receptors are the taste receptors that are present on the surface of the tongue in taste buds. Each taste bud consists of many taste receptor cells. The opening of each taste bud, taste pore, allows fluid in the mouth to come in contact with receptor cells.

Option C is wrong because cutaneous receptor is the type of sensory receptor found in the skin ( the dermis or epidermis). They are a part of the somatosensory system. Cutaneous receptors include mechanoreceptors (pressure or distortion), nociceptors (pain), and thermoreceptors (temperature).
Option D is wrong because option A and C are wrong.

questions.explanation: The gustatory system or sense of taste is the sensory system that is partially responsible for the perception of taste (flavor). olfactory system, or sense of smell, is the sensory system used for smelling. Cutaneous receptors are a part of the somatosensory system including mechanoreceptors, nociceptors

It maintains homeostasis and contains centres for regulating hunger,sleep,thrist,body temperature,blood pressure etc.
It controls the automatic functions of the body.

questions.explanation: Much like a thermostat regulates the temperature inside your home, the hypothalamus regulates your body temperature, responding to internal and external stimuli and making adjustments to keep the body within one or two degrees of 98.6 degrees.

questions.text: Diameter of an artery can be changed by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The diameter of any given vessel may also change frequently throughout the day in response to neural and chemical signals that trigger vasodilation and vasoconstriction.
The diameter of any given vessel may also change frequently throughout the day in response to neural and chemical signals that trigger vasodilation and vasoconstriction.

Arteries are capable of changing their diameter or size due to various factors. Both nervous and chemical stimulation can cause vasoconstriction (narrowing) or vasodilation (widening) of arteries. Nervous stimulation of the sympathetic nervous system can cause vasoconstriction by releasing norepinephrine, while chemical stimulation can be caused by substances like angiotensin II, which can also lead to vasoconstriction. On the other hand, substances like nitric oxide can cause vasodilation by relaxing smooth muscles in the arterial walls. Therefore, the correct option is "Both chemical and nervous stimulation".
The diameter of any given vessel may also change frequently throughout the day in response to neural and chemical signals that trigger vasodilation and vasoconstriction.

questions.explanation: The diameter of any given vessel may also change frequently throughout the day in response to neural and chemical signals that trigger vasodilation and vasoconstriction.

questions.text: Negative feedback mechanism is the characteristic of which class?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While fish do not have regulatory mechanisms to maintain homeostasis, the negative feedback mechanism is not exclusive to Class fish.
Amphibians also have regulatory mechanisms, but again, the negative feedback mechanism is not specific to Class Amphibia
Reptilia, like fish and amphibians, have regulatory mechanisms to maintain homeostasis, tbut he negative feedback mechanism is not a defining characteristic of Class Reptilia
The negative feedback mechanism is a regulatory process commonly found in mammals.

questions.explanation: Negative feedback is a type of regulation in biological systems in which the end product of a process, in turn, reduces the stimulus of that same process. Feedback, in general, is a regulatory mechanism present in many biological reactions. By allowing certain pathways to be turned off and on, the body can control various aspects of its internal environment. Examples of negative feedback include regulation of blood glucose levels and temperature regulation of the body.

questions.text: Which of the following occurs in the body in response to the secretion of Glucagon?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Conversion of glucose to glycogen in liver cells
options.explanation: Conversion of glucose to glycogen decreases blood glucose levels. It is a function of insulin.
A decrease in blood glucose level is done by insulin.

options.text: Increased uptake of glucose by muscle cells
options.explanation: Muscle cells cause glucose to be used while doing work. It is not a function of glucagon because glucagon causes an increase in the blood glucose level.

options.text: Production of cyclic AMP in target cells
options.explanation: 'Glucagon' has an opposite role to 'insulin' i.e. it acts to release blood glucose in the plasma when blood has a low level of glucose in it. When glucagon is released, it stimulates the liver to break down glycogen into glucose. The chief role of cyclic AMP in several tissues is to promote the mobilization of glucose and fatty acid reserves. In the liver, glucagon, and catecholamines cause an increase in the intracellular level of cyclic AMP by stimulating adenylyl cyclase.

questions.explanation: 'Glucagon' has the opposite role to 'insulin' i.e. it mobilizes blood glucose in the plasma when you’re hypoglycemic. When glucagon is released, it can perform the following tasks: Stimulating the liver to break down glycogen to be released into the blood as glucose. Activating gluconeogenesis is the conversion of amino acids into glucose. Breaking down stored fat (triglycerides) into fatty acids for use as fuel by cells. All of the other options, except D, explain the role of insulin and not glucagon. By simply using an elimination method, we can deduce that D is the right answer. Just a piece of additional information; The chief role of cyclic AMP in several tissues seems to be to facilitate or promote the mobilization of glucose and fatty acid reserves. In the liver, glucagon, and catecholamines cause an increase in the intracellular level of cyclic AMP by stimulating adenylyl cyclase.

questions.text: The correct option about spinal nerves is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Spinal nerves have a total of 62 nerves i.e., 31 pairs of nerves.
All the spinal nerves are mixed nerves.

options.text: The dorsal root contains sensory neurons
options.explanation: This option is correct. It is true that the dorsal root contains sensory neurons.

options.text: The ventral root contains motor neurons
options.explanation: This option is also correct. It is true that the ventral root contains motor neurons.
Since both option C and D are correct, Hence the Option E (all) is the most suitable option.

questions.explanation: As the dorsal root emerges from the intervertebral neural foramina, it forms the dorsal root ganglion (DRG). The DRG contains the cell body of the sensory neurons. These neurons relay sensory neural messages from the periphery to the central nervous system (brain and spinal cord). The ventral (anterior) or motor root consists of axons from the lower motor neurons in the ventral horn of the spinal cord. The ventral roots typically have fewer fibers than the dorsal roots, but they are of a larger caliber because they contain thick axons originating from the large lower motor neurons.

questions.text: _ is sometimes given by injection as an emergency treatment in cardiac arrest.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acetylcholine is the neurotransmitter at neuromuscular junctions, at synapses in the ganglia of the visceral motor system. It is a major neurotransmitter outside the CNS i.e., in the PNS.

Dopamine is a chemical released in the brain that makes you feel good. Having the right amount of dopamine is important both for your body and your brain. Dopamine helps nerve cells to send messages to each other.

Serotonin is a chemical that carries messages between nerve cells in the brain and throughout your body. Serotonin plays a key role in such body functions as mood, sleep, digestion, nausea, wound healing, bone health and blood clotting.
Epinephrine is the primary drug administered during cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) to reverse cardiac arrest. Epinephrine increases arterial blood pressure and coronary perfusion during CPR.

questions.explanation: When CPR does not generate coronary perfusion of more than 15-20 mmHg, return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) rarely or never occurs. After more than a few minutes of cardiac arrest, arterial tone collapses and epinephrine or another vasoconstrictor is essential for restoration of cardiac activity.

questions.text: Which one of the following is a neurotransmitter?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Dopamine is a neurotransmitter made in your brain. It plays a role as a “reward center” and in many body functions, including memory, movement, motivation, mood, attention, etc.

Hydrochloric acid plays an important role in your digestion and immunity. It helps break down protein and absorb essential nutrients, and it helps control viruses and bacteria that might otherwise infect your stomach.
Sodium ion (Na+) is the principal extracellular cation and solute and is essential for generation of action potentials in nervous and cardiac tissue.
Calcium ions (Ca+) trigger contraction by reaction with regulatory proteins that in the absence of calcium prevent interaction of actin and myosin.

questions.explanation: 'Dopamine' is a neurotransmitter that helps send signals in the brain. Neurotransmitters are chemicals made by nerve cells called neurons. They're used to communicate messages across different parts of the brain and between the brain and the rest of the body. 'Dopamine' is involved mainly in controlling movement.

questions.text: Which hormone is chemically a steroid?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: ADH or Vasopressin is essentially a polypeptide hormone.
Thyroxine is essentially an amino acid derivative.
Cortisone is essentially a steroid hormone.
Insulin is essentially a protein hormone.

questions.explanation: Steroid hormones are fat-soluble molecules made from cholesterol. Among these are the three major sex hormone groups: estrogens, androgens, and progesterones. Males and females make all three, just in different amounts. The steroids that are made almost exclusively in the adrenal glands are cortisol, 11-deoxycortisol, aldosterone, corticosterone, and 11-deoxycorticosterone. Most other steroid hormones, including estrogens, are made by the adrenal glands and the gonads.

questions.text: Which brain portion is responsible for controlling body coordination?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The medulla oblongata helps control vital processes like your heartbeat, breathing, and blood pressure.
The main job of the amygdala is to regulate emotions, such as fear and aggression. The amygdala is also involved in tying emotional meaning to our memories, reward processing, and decision-making.
The cerebellum is part of your brain that helps coordinate and regulate a wide range of functions and processes in both your brain and body. The cerebellum controls balance and movement and posture.
The pons is a part of your brainstem, a structure that links your brain to your spinal cord. It handles unconscious processes such as your sleep-wake cycle and breathing.

questions.explanation: The cerebellum, in the back of the brain, controls balance, coordination, and fine muscle control (e.g., walking). It also functions to maintain posture and equilibrium.

questions.text: The estrogen hormone secretion during the oogenesis is stimulated by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When the level of estrogen is sufficiently high, it produces a sudden release of LH, usually around day thirteen of the cycle.

Inhibin is a protein secreted by the Sertoli cells in men and by the granulosa cells in women respectively. It inhibits the synthesis and release of the follicle-stimulating hormone in the pituitary gland and reduces the hypothalamic LH - releasing hormone content.

As the follicle grows, blood levels of estrogen rise significantly by cycle day seven. This increase in estrogen begins to inhibit the secretion of FSH. The fall in FSH allows smaller follicles to die off.

Testosterone is a male sex hormone, or androgen, produced in a woman's ovaries in small amounts. Combined with estrogen, the female sex hormone, testosterone helps with the growth, maintenance, and repair of a woman's reproductive tissues, bone mass, and human behaviors.

questions.explanation: Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), the hormone responsible for starting follicle (egg) development, causes the level of estrogen, the primary female hormone, to rise.

questions.text: Reabsorption of calcium is triggered by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Aldosterone triggers reabsorption of sodium. Aldosterone is a hormone produced by the adrenal glands, which are small glands located on top of each kidney. It is classified as a mineralocorticoid hormone and plays a crucial role in regulating the balance of electrolytes and fluid in the body.

Parathormone triggers reabsorption of calcium. Parathormone, also known as parathyroid hormone (PTH), is a hormone produced by the parathyroid glands, which are small glands located near the thyroid gland in the neck. Parathormone plays a key role in regulating calcium and phosphate levels in the blood and bone.

ADH triggers reabsorption of waterAntidiuretic hormone (ADH), also known as vasopressin, is a hormone produced by the hypothalamus and released by the posterior pituitary gland. ADH plays a crucial role in regulating the body's water balance and urine production.

Vasopressin is another name for ADH Vasopressin, also known as antidiuretic hormone (ADH), is a hormone produced by the hypothalamus and released by the posterior pituitary gland. It plays a crucial role in regulating blood pressure and fluid balance in the body.

questions.explanation: Aldosterone triggers reabsorption of sodium Parathormone triggers reabsorption of calcium ADH triggers reabsorption of water. Vasopressin is another name for ADH.

questions.text: The _ may be a cell, neuron ending or an organ that receives the stimulus.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It receives the signal to execute a function in response to the stimulus.

It receives the stimulus and sends it to the central nervous system. Receptors in neurons are specialized structures or proteins that detect and respond to specific stimuli in the environment or within the body. They play a crucial role in the sensory perception and transmission of signals in the nervous system.
It is not a relevant term.

Stimulus is a signal that is received by a receptor and causes a response Stimuli in neurons refer to the various external or internal factors that elicit a response or activity in neurons. Neurons are highly specialized cells that are capable of detecting and responding to different types of stimuli, allowing for the transmission and processing of information in the nervous system.

questions.explanation: Sensory neurons, also known as afferent neurons, are neurons in the nervous system that convert a specific type of stimulus, via their receptors, into action potentials or graded potentials. This process is called sensory transduction.

questions.text: The _ is the main nutritional part and it's concerned with the biosynthesis of materials necessary for neurons.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nerves are like cables that carry electrical impulses between your brain and the rest of your bodyNerves are bundles of specialized cells called neurons that transmit electrical signals throughout the body. Neurons are the fundamental building blocks of the nervous system, which includes the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves.

Axon is a nerve fibre which carries signals from the cell body to the pre synaptic membraneThe axon is a long, slender projection of a neuron that carries electrical signals, known as action potentials, away from the cell body towards other neurons, muscle cells, or glandular cells. It is a crucial component of the nervous system and plays a vital role in transmitting information and coordinating various physiological processes.

Soma is the cell body of the nerve cell which contains most of the cell organelles The soma, also known as the cell body or perikaryon, is the main component of a neuron that contains the nucleus and other essential organelles. It serves as the metabolic and genetic center of the neuron, playing a crucial role in maintaining its overall function and survival.

Dendrites are nerve fibres which carry signals from the postsynaptic membrane/receptor to the cell bodyDendrites are the branched extensions of a neuron that receive incoming signals from other neurons or sensory receptors. They are an essential part of the neuron's structure and function, serving as the primary site for synaptic input and information integration.

questions.explanation: Also known as a soma, the cell body is the neuron's core. The cell body carries genetic information, maintains the neuron's structure, and provides energy to drive activities. Like other cell bodies, a neuron's soma contains a nucleus and specialized organelles.

questions.text: Direction of reflex action is from:

options.text: Receptor to sensory neuron to motor neuron and then through associative neurons to effect
options.explanation: Signal passes through associative neurons before reaching motor neurons

options.text: Receptor to sensory neuron to associative neuron and then through motor neurons to effector

options.text: Effector to sensory neuron to associative neuron and then through motor neurons to Receptor
options.explanation: Stimulus is received by a receptor

options.text: Effector to sensory neuron to motor neuron and then through associative neurons to Receptor
options.explanation: Stimulus is received by a receptor
